content.full_text:
October 10, 1990

1101- 2nd Street
Hermosa Beach, CA 90254

SCOTT HALLABRIN
ACTING GENERAL COUNSEL
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: NO. 90-563

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

The purpose of this letter is to inquire as to the status of this advise inquire regarding a rent/gift issue. You acknowledging receipt of my original letter on September 7, 1990, and assigned it the above referenced number. On September 17, 1990 I wrote you again with a few additional questions on this same issue in order to completely resolve current, past and future reporting requirements. I have not received an acknowledgement of my supplemental questions. I am attaching a copy of this letter for your reference.

I realize the obligation to promptly comply with reporting requirements, however, this particular rent/gift issue is one that I never came across in any written materials in my five years as City Clerk, nor is our current City Attorney familiar with it. I have no difficulty in declaring a Conflict of Interest in being a month-to-month tenant and assuming that the rent could possibly be over $250.00 under "fair market value" for an entire years period. This would result in a tenant basically having the same presumption of a conflict as an owner. This seems fair and reasonable.

The difficulty that I am facing is one of determining an estimate of the "fair market value" and calculating an exact dollar figure for the amount of the "gift" as a result of the amount of rent vs. the fair market value. This seems to put a renter in a higher level of disclosure than a homeowner.

I have always had the understanding that an official's personal residence is not disclosable under the Act. An official could own a multi million dollar home and not be required to report the current fair market value, what his financing arrangements are, if he negotiated a "below fair market value" on the purchase price, or if the seller carrier back paper at a below market interest rate. My understanding is that it does not need to be disclosed because it involves an ownership of his personal residence.

Yet with your recent determination that difference of actual rent and fair market value of rent is a thing of "value" and therefore a reportable "gift" requires the official to make an estimate of fair market value and deduct the actual rent paid from this to determine the exact amount of the gift. As a result, the official will have to publicly disclose/justify how they determined fair market value and the amount of the rent they pay.

Rather than being required to calculate an exact amount of the difference of rent vs. fair market value to report the gift (thing of value) which is so subjective and leaves the official always subject to challenge by political adversaries as to how the calculation was made, perhaps a method could be imposed to allow an exception to the requirement that the exact amount of gift be disclosed, and that it could be reported as "over $250.00" for this issue only.

I assume that the purpose of this rent/gift issue is to determine if a potential conflict exists. What an exact calculation of the amount of gift results in is to require an official who happens not to be wealthy enough to be a homeowner to have a higher level of disclosure requirement regarding their personal residence than an owner. Allowing the "over $250.00" disclosure to be accepted in this situation seems to accomplish this purpose and leave a renter at a similar disclosure level as that of a homeowner

I hope that you will give consideration to these resulting consequences
of your recent determination and perhaps consider some adjustments.

I appreciate your assistance in this matter and look forward to a fair
and reasonable resolution of this issue.

Sincerely,

[signature]
KATHLEEN MIDSTOKKE

September 17, 1990


1101- 2nd Street
Hermosa Beach, CA  90254

SCOTT HALLABRIN
ACTING GENERAL COUNSEL
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am in receipt of your letter dated September 7, 1990
acknowledging receipt of my letter No. 90-563 requesting
advice regarding a recent advice letter to the City Attorney
of Hermosa Beach by the Commission that any rent paid by a
month-to-month tenant "below fair market value" is
determined to be a "gift" to that person.

As indicated in my letter previously, I raised several
questions regarding a reasonable method for determining fair
market value of rent in order to determine the proper amount
of gift which must be reported.  A few other questions have
arise and I hope you will be able to answer them also to
resolve this issue completely at this time.

1.  What date did or does this determination that
"below fair market value" is a "gift" go or goes into
effect?  I ask this question because prior to being elected
to City Council in November, 1989, I was City Clerk
since 1984 and was required to file 730 Forms, which might
need to be amended for this "gift" disclosure.

2.  Enclosed are the disclosure categories for the
adopted Conflict of Interest Code for the City of Hermosa
Beach.  City Clerk was required to disclose categories
1,2,3,4 .  Would this "below market value rent"/"gift"
disclosure apply to these categories?

3.  For future consideration, does the "below market
value rent"/"gift" disclosure apply to month-to-month
tenancy only?  Would it apply if I leased a house for one to
five years?

SCOTT HALLABRIN
September 17, 1990
Page Two


4. It is my understanding that there is a new $1000.00
limitation on the amount of a gift an official can receive
from a single source within a year. When would this apply
to my situation? Does it mean that I cannot have over
$1000.00 in below fair market value rent for the calendar
year 1990, to be reported in April, 1991?

I would appreciate your attempting to address these
additional questions so that this issue can be completely
resolved. I can amend any previous forms if required, and
disclose this item to your satisfaction in the future.

Thank you for your assistance in regards to this
matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
KATHLEEN MIDSTOKE
